The witnesses agree the chapter opens with the alarm of the Canaanite king at Israel's advance and Gibeon's defection: Adonizedek of Jerusalem "heard how Joshua had taken Ai... and how... Gibeon had made peace"; the name Adonizedek — "lord of righteousness," king of Salem — stands as a dark counterpart to Melchizedek of old, the righteous priest-king (Heb. 7:1); the king's alarm shows how the report of God's work strikes fear into his enemies; it teaches that the progress of God's kingdom unsettles the powers that oppose it, the advance of Israel and the going-over of Gibeon rousing the doomed city to its last resistance.
